title: Who is Andy Burnham's wife, Marie-France van Heel?
description: Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Marie-France van Heel was at her husband's side as he became Labour leader on FridayByJennifer McKiernanPolit
body: Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Marie-France van Heel was at her husband's side as he became Labour leader on FridayByJennifer McKiernanPolitical reporterUpdated 57 minutes agoShe was described as the coolest girl in college when she first started dating Andy Burnham.More than three decades later, Marie-France van Heel is about to walk into Downing Street alongside her husband to take on one of the most powerful unofficial roles in government - the prime minister's spouse.Burnham will replace Sir Keir Starmer in No 10 on Monday, and he met Dutch-born van Heel, known as Frankie, when she started studying at Fitzwilliam College at the University of Cambridge in 1989.He was in his second year of an English Literature degree, and Burnham's former lecturer, John Mullan, wrote in the Times that other students thought he had scooped the pools when they began dating.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Van Heel and Burnham during his 2017 Greater Manchester mayor campaignVan Heel was born in January 1970, the same month as Burnham, and grew up in the Netherlands and Belgium before moving to the UK.Although they started dating at university, van Heel had a surprising request in the early days of their relationship, when she asked Burnham if she could go on Cilla Black's TV dating show Blind Date - and he agreed.So in 1992, wearing flares, a bottle green jacket and a yellow headband over her bobbed blonde hair, van Heel appeared on the hit ITV programme.She chose Will from Surrey to be her date on a holiday to Gibraltar, but the pair did not hit it off.Having quickly reunited, Burnham and van Heel were reportedly in a bar in Parliament 12 years later when Will walked in, having become the director of communications for the Conservative Party. Image source, Shutterstock/ ITVImage caption, Van Heel appeared on the ITV show Blind Date, hosted by Cilla Black, in 1992By this point, van Heel had given birth to their first child, Jimmy - and it was his birth in March 2000 which Burnham has said spurred them on to get married, after years of living together."I think marriage is better for kids," Burnham told the Daily Telegraph in 2007, external, adding: "I'm a bit old fashioned on these matters - although I have to admit that Jimmy, my eldest son, was at our wedding so I'm only old fashioned up to a point. "When my wife got pregnant for the first time it was a complete shock. My first thought was, 'Oh my God, we'll have to get married'."Their daughters, Rosie and Annie, were born a few years later while the couple were living in Manchester.Rosie and Jimmy work for drinks brand Jägermeister and the Royal College of Nursing respectively, while Annie has just graduated from Cardiff University. Neither van Heel nor the children are expected to move into Downing Street, but nothing has been confirmed about where their rescue dog Axel will live. Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Andy Burnham and Marie-France van Heel have been together since their university daysImage source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Burnham celebrates winning the Greater Manchester mayoral election in 2017 with son Jimmy (L) wife Marie-France van Heel (2nd-L) daughter Rosie (3rd-L) and daughter Annie (4th-R) and his mum Eileen (R).Van Heel has worked in marketing and strategy for many years, notably for Sky, and has also been involved in the logo designs for the BBC and England Rugby.That professional experience is credited by some for helping design Burnham's striking campaign logos, external, which feature a cartoon of the northerner in his trademark black t-shirt and black NHS-style glasses.There has also been a small controversy over van Heel's current role as the chief marketing officer of Iduna, which owns large parts of Manchester's electric vehicle charging network.As Greater Manchester Mayor, Burnham had declared his wife's job and stepped away from all decisions about Iduna and any linked infrastructure to avoid any perception of a conflict of interest.The Conservatives complained he had not declared she held 0.2% of company shares, the Times , externalreported, but the Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA) confirmed Burnham was given legal advice that no further declaration was necessary because he had already declared Van Heel as an employee."We are satisfied that the mayor has met his legal obligations to register disclosable pecuniary interests," a GCMA spokesperson said.

schema.headline: Who is Andy Burnham's wife, Marie-France van Heel?

title: Hope Street: The small seaside town winning big from TV tourism
description: Kathryn Strain, who is a member of the Chunky Dunkers sea swimming group in Donaghadee, says it's common to see tourists from cruise ships visiting Be
body: Kathryn Strain, who is a member of the Chunky Dunkers sea swimming group in Donaghadee, says it's common to see tourists from cruise ships visiting Belfast making trips to the town."You can hear them saying, 'oh, that's where the police station was, and oh, this is so and so's house'," so it has been great, it has been good for the town, definitely," she told BBC News NI.Some of the Hope Street cast and crew have also braved the cold of Irish Sea and enjoyed - or maybe not - a dip.Like many locals, Kathryn can be spotted in Hope Street episodes as an extra."I think my most famous [part] was, and as my husband says I've got the face for a funeral, I was in it at a funeral," she jokes.Her friend, Bridie McElhill, who also swims with the Chunky Dunkers, says the show has helped put Donaghadee "on the map"."People come to Donaghadee now to see the town and see all the locations where the filming has taken place."I do think it has brought people into the town, and that's great for the town."

schema.headline: Hope Street: The small seaside town winning big from TV tourism

title: World Cup 2026: Kylian Mbappe becomes first player to win Golden Boot twice as Rodri named best player
description: Kylian Mbappe has become the first player to win the World Cup Golden Boot twice after earning the award for top scorer at the 2026 tournament.The Fra
body: Kylian Mbappe has become the first player to win the World Cup Golden Boot twice after earning the award for top scorer at the 2026 tournament.The France forward netted 10 goals in eight games to edge out Lionel Messi in a high-quality race.Having struck eight times to finish top scorer at Qatar 2022, Mbappe has won the Golden Boot in back-to-back World Cups.The 27-year-old was the first man to reach double figures for World Cup goals since Gerd Muller for West Germany in 1970.Only Just Fontaine, who hit 13 for France in 1958, and Hungary's Sandor Kocsis with 11 in 1954, have scored more individual goals in a single tournament.Mbappe is also now the men's World Cup all-time top scorer with 22 goals - one ahead of Messi.World champions Spain swept the rest of the awards, including captain Rodri winning the Golden Ball as the tournament's best player.Mbappe moved clear of Messi on Saturday with two goals in the bronze match, although he could not stop France falling to a 6-4 defeat against England and finishing fourth."When you score that many goals in the World Cup, it certainly takes you to a whole new level," he said after Saturday's match in Miami."But I would have preferred not to be the top scorer and to have played in the final. It's good for my legacy once I've retired, but right now, that's not what's on my mind."Mbappe also scored twice against Senegal and Iraq in the group stage, as well as Sweden in the last 32.He followed that with strikes against Paraguay in the last 16 - the 70th-minute winner from the penalty spot - and against Morocco in the quarter-finals.Messi could have overtaken the Real Madrid forward with a hat-trick in the World Cup final on Sunday.However, the 39-year-old could not find the net in New Jersey as his Argentina side were beaten 1-0 by Spain, and ended the competition with eight goals in as many matches.England's Jude Bellingham and Erling Haaland of Norway scored seven times, while France forward Ousmane Dembele and English captain Harry Kane followed them in the list with six.

schema.headline: World Cup 2026: Kylian Mbappe becomes first player to win Golden Boot twice as Rodri named best player

title: Norway opens new memorial for victims of 2011 massacre
description: Norway has opened a new memorial for the 77 victims of the country's deadliest terror attack in peacetime, days before its 15th anniversary.On 22 July
body: Norway has opened a new memorial for the 77 victims of the country's deadliest terror attack in peacetime, days before its 15th anniversary.On 22 July 2011, neo-Nazi Anders Breivik killed eight people with a car bomb in Oslo and shot dead another 69 - mostly teenagers - at a youth camp on Utoeya island. Hundreds more were injured in Norway's worst atrocity in recent history.On Sunday, survivors and relatives of the victims gathered in Oslo for the unveiling of a large artwork showing a bird native to Utoeya island.The artist said the work was two-fold - to remember the victims and to engage the public in an act of democratic resilience against far-right ideologies that inspired the attack.The contemporary artwork features a huge blue steel frame supporting a 12-metre (39ft) high stone mosaic that depicts a small wading bird, as well as straw and sticks reflected in the water.Called Upholding, the memorial is located in Oslo's renovated government district, close to the location of the bomb blast that started the 2011 attack.The artist Matias Faldbakken said he had seen the bird on Utoeya island. Many of those killed on the island were teenagers involved with the Norwegian Labour Party's youth wing, the AUF.The names of all 77 victims are engraved on the memorial."July 22, 2011 was a brutal attack not only on individuals, but also on our democracy," said Merete Stamneshagen, head of a support group for victims and survivors, quoted by the AFP news agency.Norway's Crown Prince Haakon and government officials also attended the inauguration ceremony in Oslo on Sunday.Haakon also visited the new 22 July Centre - Norway's national memorial and education centre dedicated to the attacks.In 2012, Breivik was sentenced to 21 years in jail, the maximum a court in Norway can impose, though it can be extended for as long as he is deemed a threat. Over the years, Breivik has challenged the terms of his sentence.In 2016, he partially won his human rights case against Norway before it was overturned the following year.In 2022, he failed in his bid for parole, with the court ruling he had not changed and remained a risk to society. In 2024, he unsuccessfully sued the government over claims of isolation, as he is kept separately from other prisoners for security reasons.

schema.headline: Norway opens new memorial for victims of 2011 massacre

title: Burnham is fifth PM in four years - now he'll try to buck the trend
description: By lunchtime today, the UK will have its fifth prime minister in four years.No other sentence can so pithily summarise the nature of British politics
body: By lunchtime today, the UK will have its fifth prime minister in four years.No other sentence can so pithily summarise the nature of British politics right now than that one.In the summer of 2022 Boris Johnson was on his way out, but still in office. Since then there has been Liz Truss, Rishi Sunak and Sir Keir Starmer.Little wonder Andy Burnham will say later, we are told, that he is "acutely conscious" of this churn at the top. His critics, within the Labour Party and beyond it, may allow themselves a wry smile at that, given they would argue he has been agitating for this moment for ages.In the post-pandemic, post-Brexit 2020s, when for so long so many have felt like life is getting harder, the national mood is impatient and political support is fragmenting.The consequence of that in recent years has meant those in government have been unpopular.That is the backdrop to Burnham's arrival in Downing Street, just as it was for Sir Keir Starmer 746 days ago - on another sunny July day, just two years back.That trend of unpopular governments is the thing Burnham is now trying to buck.He is determined to hit the ground running and, where possible, to maintain the style of leadership he adopted as the mayor of Greater Manchester.The rules of the House of Commons and meetings with the King, among others, will probably mean he wears a suit more often than he has in recent years.But other conventions may be very intentionally ditched. I hear, for instance, that he may not bother with a lectern for his first speech as prime minister, outside 10 Downing Street, on Monday.I also hear he is keen on question and answer sessions with voters around the country - "People's Question Time" as one ally put it.Before Burnham's big moment will come Sir Keir's departure.Having spent his final weekend in office at Chequers, the prime minister's country retreat in Buckinghamshire, Sir Keir will give a brief address - with a lectern - before heading to see the King to stand down as prime minister.I expect he will want to talk up what he sees as his achievements at home, as well as those on the world stage. His team talk with particular pride about getting immigration and NHS waiting lists in England down, lifting children out of poverty and introducing a social media ban for teenage children.I expect there will be a modest gathering of staff and supporters to see him off. And there will be some overlap between the supporters of the departing prime minister and the supporters of his successor - but, as you would expect, some there for one bit and not for the other.The afternoon and evening will bring news of appointments to the cabinet, including, we hear, a cabinet minister whose focus will be on artificial intelligence.A keen eye will be on who gets the big jobs - chancellor, home secretary and foreign secretary. Shabana Mahmood, Wes Streeting, Ed Miliband, Angela Rayner, Lucy Powell to name just five - which roles do they get, and what do the appointments say about how Burnham intends to govern? We will soon find out.We will also begin to get a sense of the new prime minister's immediate priorities: the cost of living and energy bills among them, and how his new government will work. To what extent will the No 10 operation be beefed up, and how much clout will No 10 North, Burnham's office in Manchester, have?What beckons in the coming hours is no longer novel, given the turnover of prime ministers recently, but it is no less significant for its increased frequency.What we learn in the coming hours will have a big bearing on how we are governed in the coming weeks, months and years.

schema.headline: Burnham is fifth PM in four years - now he'll try to buck the trend

title: Welsh Water tells Pontycymer residents to pay £7,000 to fix water pipe leak
description: Residents say they could face prosecution if they do not pay thousands of pounds to repair a water pipe outside their homes.The residents of five hous
body: Residents say they could face prosecution if they do not pay thousands of pounds to repair a water pipe outside their homes.The residents of five houses in Pontycymer, Bridgend county, said they were told by Welsh Water that a leak from the shared pipe supplying their homes needs to be repaired at their own cost, which they estimate could cost more than £7,000 each.The Garw Valley locals claim they have been given no evidence of where the leak is which their insurance companies need to cover the cost, causing sleepless nights. A Welsh Water spokesperson said it does not own or manage the pipe so it's the responsibility of property owners, and residents have 11 days to fix it.Welsh Water added that the leak needed to be repaired as it was wasting water, and it could issue a potential prosecution under Section 75 Notice under the Water Industry Act 1991, external.The estimated cost of more than £7,000 would go towards replacing the rear water pipe in their back gardens with a new pipe at the front of the terraced row, they said, along with connecting it to each house individually.Resident Nerys Walters said the dispute had brought her massive amounts of stress over the last two years.She said they initially thought the cost of repairs could be covered through insurance, but with no evidence of the leak being provided, insurers have said they would not pay out.Walters said: "It's been a nightmare situation for us that has really caused us all a lot of stress. "Welsh Water have said they would pay for a new line that runs parallel to the front of the property, but we would then have to fund the cost of it coming in from the gate, and through the house to our rear stopcock.Walters said she had spoken to "numerous contractors" who quoted the residents more than £1,000 a metre for the work."This has been going on for about a year and a half now and we've been asking for that evidence ever since but have been given nothing", she said."No evidence of the leak, and no reason as to why they adopted other pipes in the street but not ours. All the while the deadline they have given runs down. It's been very difficult and we just want to get it sorted."Bridgend councillor Martyn Jones said it was a "distressing" time for the Pontycymer residents.Jones said he had met with an engineer from Welsh Water, on behalf of the residents but claimed he was "yet to see any evidence that one or more householders are liable"."The course of action currently being undertaken by Dwr Cymru concerns me in that I question how many other residents living in the Garw Valley and beyond are equally vulnerable if a similar situation was to develop elsewhere."Enforcement and prosecution should be the last resort. I appeal to Dwr Cymru to reconsider its position and work with local residents especially those who are financially vulnerable," he added.A Welsh Water spokesperson said: "We have been working with the residents at Gwaun Bant, Pontycymer, Bridgend, who have a leak on a private, shared pipe to their properties."The pipe is not owned or managed by Dwr Cymru Welsh Water and is the responsibility of the property owners. However, to support the residents we have offered a free connection to our main if each property connects individually."We appreciate this will be an inconvenience for customers so we have also offered a contribution towards the cost of the required work to get a pipe from the properties to the main."

schema.headline: Welsh Water tells Pontycymer residents to pay £7,000 to fix water pipe leak

title: World Cup 2026: Spain's 'victory for football' over 'embarrassing' Argentina
description: Figure caption, Spain dominate Argentina to win World Cup after extra timeByEmma SmithBBC Sport journalistDescribing a result as a 'victory for footba
body: Figure caption, Spain dominate Argentina to win World Cup after extra timeByEmma SmithBBC Sport journalistDescribing a result as a 'victory for football' might seem like a trite cliche - but you won't find many neutrals disagreeing with that description of Spain's win in the 2026 World Cup final.When Ferran Torres slammed in the extra-time winner, and Rodri lifted the trophy high in New Jersey, it is hard to imagine many will have had sympathy for Argentina.Spain triumphed in a battle of endurance, for players and spectators, to beat the Argentines and win their second men's World Cup.On every football metric - and, some would say, morality too - this was the right result."There is no doubt that the right team won," former England captain Alan Shearer said on BBC One. "From start to finish, Spain controlled possession. They were the ones looking to play football, create chances and get in behind."This is a result for football because of the way that they have played. Spain deserve it."Spain had 65% possession, an expected goals tally of 1.94 and 20 shots on goal - 11 on target.At the other end, goalkeeper Unai Simon kept his seventh clean sheet in eight matches as he won the Golden Glove for the tournament's best goalkeeper.Spain conceded just one goal at the 2026 World Cup - the fewest conceded by any winning team.Spain pass Argentina in every metricFigure caption, Ferran Torres finally gives Spain a lead against ArgentinaSpain are - even in the hard-to-quantify world of international football - clearly the preeminent side in the world.They are world, Olympic and European champions - not to mention women's world champions too. The men are now unbeaten in their past 38 matches in all competitions - the longest run by any European team in history.They stumbled out of the gates in this World Cup with a 0-0 draw against Cape Verde - meaning that on both occasions Spain have won the world title they have failed to win their opener, after a 1-0 loss to Switzerland in 2010. There have only been three instances on record of a player completing 100 or more passes in a World Cup final. Two of those were Spain players on Sunday - Rodri with 101, and Pau Cubarsi with 121.And for many of the squad, this might just be the start.Cubarsi, 19, was named young player of the tournament, while it seems very likely his Barcelona team-mate Lamine Yamal has his best years ahead.At 19 years and six days, Lamine Yamal became the third-youngest player to play in a World Cup final, after Pele in 1958 and Giuseppe Bergomi in 1982. He is now the third-youngest winner.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Enzo Fernandez was sent off in the 90th minute following a late challenge on CubarsiArgentina fail to spoil Spain's partyArgentina are so often painted as the pantomime villains of international football - particularly by England fans.As they did in the semi-final against the Three Lions, they appeared in the first half to be more interested in stopping any semblance of football occurring than playing the game themselves.Leandro Paredes was the spoiler in chief, with Enzo Fernandez his deputy.Though four Argentina players were booked, many will feel that adds further to the theory they have had lenient treatment from officials at this tournament.Argentina made a total of 464 passes, compared to Spain's 852.But, unlike England, Spain did not wilt in the second half. Had they won this game long before Torres' 106th-minute strike there could have been few complaints.Argentina's Emiliano Martinez had to make 11 saves - the most by any goalkeeper on record in a World Cup final.At the other end, meanwhile, the defending champions became the first team in World Cup history not to have a shot in 90 minutes of a final. Their first attempt came via Lionel Messi in the 117th minute.Argentina lost their crown, and the scenes that followed the final whistle led many to feel they had lost their dignity too.'Disgusting behaviour'Figure caption, Fight breaks out between Spain and Argentina players at full-timeFernandez was first shown a yellow card for dissent, then a second for a spectacularly late challenge that sent Cubarsi somersaulting.After the full-time whistle, Paredes was involved in scraps with Rodri, Gavi and Borja Iglesias and team mate Nahuel Molina was seen throwing a punch at a passing Spain player too."Embarrassing, seeing that sort of stuff after the game has been played," said BBC pundit Wayne Rooney. "Someone has been crowned World Cup champions. It is not good enough."Paredes is better than that. It doesn't stop there. He keeps going and going."Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni attempted to defend his side's performance through floods of tears at his post-match media conference, but could only admit the best team had won."Even though they were out-playing us until the 85th minute, I would love to know what would have happened had we still had 11 men on the pitch," he said of Fernandez's dismissal."We managed to go all the way to the end when the match was so open and it just couldn't be. They deserved the victory, that is the reality."Argentina might have taken the World Cup final to extra time - the fifth of the past six to go that far - but that could not mask their inferiority."Argentina can have absolutely no complaints about what happened," former England goalkeeper Joe Hart told BBC One. "Disgusting behaviour."I don't think Rodri needed to get in their faces but you understand that after 120 minutes where you have been wound up and you thought you've been wronged, and the referee has been on your back."

schema.headline: World Cup 2026: Spain's 'victory for football' over 'embarrassing' Argentina

title: Marcus Rashford: What does the future hold for Manchester United forward?
description: Marcus Rashford's World Cup ended with a considered and thoughtful social media post, issued in the aftermath of England's amazing 6-4 third-place pla
body: Marcus Rashford's World Cup ended with a considered and thoughtful social media post, issued in the aftermath of England's amazing 6-4 third-place play-off victory over France in Miami.Disappointments are guaranteed in football, he said, before opening out in a more positive vein and stating firmly: "Keep believing in us. Our time will come."He added: "Now it's time for me to rest, reflect, spend some quality time with family and friends, and come back a better version of myself."'Reflect' is the big word in that overview.At 28, Rashford is moving into what should be the prime years of his career.He has just won his first league title, La Liga with Barcelona, has 77 England caps and featured in six out of eight games as Thomas Tuchel's side delivered the country's best World Cup performance since 1966.But in reality, a large part of what Rashford will be reflecting on with his family - some of whom are also key figures in terms of guiding his career - is whether his club future lies at Manchester United.Rashford is contracted to Manchester United until 2028. His salary of £325,000 a week makes him the club's top earner at a time when minority owner Sir Jim Ratcliffe is trying to stay on top of salary costs, which are, by some distance, the club's highest expenditure.Barcelona had an option, in the loan deal they agreed for Rashford last season, to turn it into a permanent transfer for £26m. As expected, that deadline passed in the middle of June without it being activated.There was another clause in Rashford's contract that allowed other clubs - Liverpool and Manchester City excepted - to buy him for £40m, which expired earlier in July. As expected, no-one triggered that either.Sources close to the player felt it was illogical to think a club would come in and offer more than Barcelona would have had to pay.With England's World Cup campaign at an end, Rashford is now entitled to a three-week break.Unless the timings are altered, or his situation changes, he will return to Manchester United just as they begin a six-day training camp in the Republic of Ireland, scheduled around an historic meeting with old rivals Leeds at Croke Park on 12 August.Suggestions around Rashford have been that he is relaxed about the prospect of returning to training at United.For a start, there is not the same confrontational relationship between the player and his coach.Ruben Amorim fell out with the forward within weeks of his arrival from Sporting Lisbon. Amorim eventually exiled him, which triggered a loan move to Aston Villa. Then the head coach put Rashford in his 'bomb squad', along with Alejandro Garnacho, Antony, Tyrell Malacia and Jadon Sancho, when pre-season training began last summer. The other four players have all now left the club.Amorim's replacement, Michael Carrick, is a former team-mate of Rashford's. He has also been his coach and was, briefly, his manager, following Ole Gunnar Solskjaer's dismissal in 2021. The pair are said to get on well.Carrick's only public statement around Rashford, towards the end of last season, was conciliatory in nature, which is in keeping with his general personality. He is so far yet to be asked what his stance is around the player returning to the fold.For their part, United are sticking to their refusal to renegotiate a deal with Barcelona on reduced terms or consider a second loan to the Catalan giants. It is not clear what interest there is in Rashford at the Nou Camp given Barca have already signed England wide-man Anthony Gordon and are about to bring in Germany forward Karim Adeyemi from Borussia Dortmund.The consistency of United's messaging is being articulated in a summer where they are keen to prove they will not be bullied into overpaying to get deals over the line. That financial discipline may have consequences - while the feeling was Elliot Anderson would have joined Manchester City anyway, United did not countenance paying the £116m Nottingham Forest eventually sold him for.The same is true of Mateus Fernandes and his £85m switch from West Ham to Tottenham.Nevertheless, Manchester United's enhanced used of data through director Michael Sansoni and his dedicated team means the club feel bullish about their chances of landing alternative targets who offer better value.Clearly just as United need active sellers to buy from, they need buyers so they can offload those players they do not want.In Rashford's case, there are a limited number of clubs globally who could match his current salary. And where a club are interested, the player has to want to join them.Judging by his recent social media post, he would not do anything to jeopardise his England place. BBC Sport has also been told of some Premier League clubs he would not be keen to move to.So where will Rashford be when the transfer window closes on 1 September? Currently, the likeliest answer, by some distance, is Manchester United.After that: Any Spanish club, any Turkish club, any German club. The vagueness there underlines the current lack of market for the 15th highest scorer in United's history, with 138 goals - someone who is only 17 short of a place in the top 10.There are plenty, including former captain Gary Neville, who feel Rashford's time at Old Trafford is over and there should be no reconciliation.In the end though, it may have to happen - and not everyone would be disappointed."A fit and focused Marcus Rashford has the ability to be one of the best players," said Rashford's friend Sam Smith - the Wrexham forward who scored the winner in their pre-season victory over Carrick's team in Helsinki on Saturday - when speaking to the United We Stand podcast. "He has everything."He has had a difficult time at United and getting away from it all was a good thing."I don't know what is going to happen but as his friend, I hope he comes back and proves how good a player he was, how good he can be and starts scoring goals again."

schema.headline: Marcus Rashford: What does the future hold for Manchester United forward?

title: Fire crews battle blaze on Arthur’s Seat in Edinburgh
description: Image source, Craig Duncan PhotographyImage caption, Police closed the road surrounding Arthur's Seat as firefighters tackled the fireUpdated 1 hour a
body: Image source, Craig Duncan PhotographyImage caption, Police closed the road surrounding Arthur's Seat as firefighters tackled the fireUpdated 1 hour agoFire crews have spent the night battling a large blaze on Arthur's Seat in Edinburgh.Small plumes of smoke could still be seen on Monday morning along Salisbury Crags after the fire broke out the previous evening.Six fire engines were sent to tackle the grassfire with firefighters seen dousing the flames.The Scottish Fire and Rescue Service (SFRS) said there were "no reported casualties" but Police Scotland said Queen's Drive, the road that encircles the Holyrood Park hills, remained closed to all vehicles.It is the second fire in the last three months at the landmark, an ancient extinct volcano that offers panoramic views of the Scottish capital.  Edinburgh University student Mahek Jayantilial, who lives near Arthur's Seat, said she could see the fire from her window."I was a bit worried because I thought the fire would come this way, but thankfully there's enough concrete here to stop it," the 24-year-old told the Press Association.Figure caption, Fire crews tackle blaze on Arthur’s Seat in EdinburghJayantilial said it was "upsetting" to see Arthur's Seat on fire but she believed it was almost unavoidable as there has not been much rain recently and the grass had dried out."It was bound to happen," she added."A small bit of glass or a poorly snuffed-out cigarette butt would have ignited it, and then something so small creates such a big fire."Large swathes of vegetation below the cliff face were burned black by the fire and the sound of chainsaws could be heard on Monday morning, believed to be from work to clear vegetation. On the ground firefighters were concentrating efforts on the area of Queen's Drive closest to the Pollock Halls of Residence, the largest student accommodation site for Edinburgh University. Image source, PA MediaImage caption, Student Mahek Jayantilial said the grass had dried out from the lack of rainfallJon Henderson, SFRS assistant chief officer, said the service had dealt with a number of incidents over the course of the weekend. "We had crews at Arthur's Seat on Sunday evening and then overnight," he told BBC Radio Scotland Breakfast."At first light we stepped up that response again when the conditions were favourable for that."The feeling from the ground there is that we will probably be there for a couple of days continuing to tackle it and dampen out hotspots."Henderson said it was too early to speculate on the cause of the fire.  "Our focus at the moment is dealing with the fire and the response but there will be a full fire investigation," he said. "The difficulty we have with open fires is that they can be difficult to assess where they started because of the scale and sometimes these do happen naturally."Image gallerySkip image galleryImage source, Craig Duncan PhotographyImage caption, A large plume of smoke could be seen from as far as Fife on Sunday eveningSlide 1 of 5, An aerial shot of white plumes of smoke coming from Arthur's Seat. , A large plume of smoke could be seen from as far as Fife on Sunday eveningCairngorms wildfireIt is the latest call out for the Scottish fire service during the recent stretch of warm, dry weather which has seen a heightened risk of wildfires. Hundreds of firefighters, two helicopters and specialist resources were sent to the Cairngorms National Park where a blaze has been ongoing for four days.Six square kilometres of land had been affected resulting in homes and businesses, including campsites and a ski resort in the Glenmore Forest Park area, being evacuated as a safety precaution.A high volume pump has been used to draw water from Loch Morlich to support firefighting efforts.On Sunday, fire crews said the fire was now "surrounded" and police began escorting residents and campers back to properties and campsites to collect personal belongings.Henderson said resources remain on site but "significant progress" has been made."The activity over the rest of the day is to maintain a four kilometre long fire break and to search out and extinguish hotspots," he added.Get involvedYour news, your pictures

schema.headline: Fire crews battle blaze on Arthur’s Seat in Edinburgh

title: Are you a parent missing out on £27 a week? Here's how to check
description: Thousands more parents could receive £27 a week by remembering some paperwork shortly after their baby is born.Child benefit is an allowance that help
body: Thousands more parents could receive £27 a week by remembering some paperwork shortly after their baby is born.Child benefit is an allowance that helps with the cost of bringing up children. It is currently worth £27.05 a week for the first child, and £17.90 a week for each subsequent child.Official data shows that parents of nearly a third of children eligible for child benefit do not make a claim before their baby's first birthday.Payments can only be backdated for three months, meaning some families could be missing out on hundreds of pounds in the fog of new parenthood.The annual publication of the most popular baby names could serve as a reminder to some parents that there are other pieces of official paperwork to complete.In order to receive child benefit, which is paid every four weeks, one parent should make a claim, external. That parent will also get National Insurance credits towards their state pension.To claim, you need: Your child's birth or adoption certificateYour bank detailsThe National Insurance number for you and your partner, if you have oneChild Benefit can be claimed 48 hours after the baby's birth is registered but can only be backdated for up to three months from the date HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) receives the claim.More than 6.8 million parents received child benefit in the year to August 2025, according to HMRC data, external. But only 69% of them claimed the support before their baby's first birthday, meaning thousands of families may not getting what they are entitled to receive.The tax authority said that many of them may have chosen not to claim, owing to restrictions on high-earning parents receiving the benefit.Under the High Income Child Benefit Charge, external, payments start to reduce if one parent earns £60,000 a year. They stop entirely when one of you earns £80,000.

schema.headline: Are you a parent missing out on £27 a week? Here's how to check

title: Edward Stourton: Broadcaster and journalist dies aged 68
description: BySteven McIntoshEntertainment reporterBroadcaster and journalist Edward Stourton, whose career spanned more than 40 years including a decade on Radio
body: BySteven McIntoshEntertainment reporterBroadcaster and journalist Edward Stourton, whose career spanned more than 40 years including a decade on Radio 4's Today, has died aged 68. Stourton, who had been diagnosed with prostate cancer in 2014, died on Saturday surrounded by his family, the BBC announced in a statement. The presenter's friend and former Today co-host James Naughtie praised Stourton's "warm intelligence and wit", adding he was "one of the best colleagues". Another former Today presenter, Evan Davis, said Stourton was "a very important figure for all of us on the daily news programmes on Radio 4"."One of those people you're really glad to have around," Davis continued. "All substance, minimum ego, deeply knowledgeable, totally reliable."And he was one of the nicest of colleagues as well. He'll be missed."Presenter Mishal Husain remembered Stourton as "a true gentleman and a broadcaster of immense capacity and intellect".Figure caption, Jim Naughtie reflects on the life and legacy of broadcaster Edward Stourton, who has died aged 68.In his 2023 memoir Confessions, Stourton revealed he had been diagnosed with prostate cancer nearly a decade earlier.He had treatment but was later told it had become incurable and spread. Stourton continued to work until very recently.Stourton's career began at ITN, where he helped launch Channel 4 News and became the programme's first Washington correspondent in the latter years of the Reagan presidency.He joined the BBC as Paris correspondent in 1988, and after a period back at ITN as diplomatic editor, returned to present the One O'Clock News on BBC One. His BBC work also included Panorama, Assignment, Correspondent, as well as a phone-in programme on Radio 4, Call Ed Stourton.Stourton became a presenter of the flagship Today programme in 1999, where his immense experience as a foreign correspondent served him well while reporting from countries such as Lebanon and Afghanistan.He was also one of the first correspondents to reach New York after the 9/11 terror attacks.Image caption, Stourton in 2007 with Today co-hosts Carolyn Quinn, John Humphrys, Sarah Montague and James NaughtieHis departure from Today in 2008 was leaked to a newspaper before he had been told, upsetting Stourton and many of his colleagues.But he remained at the BBC and went on to present several other news programmes, while remaining as a contributor to Today.In recent years, as well as writing and presenting documentaries for Radio 4, Stourton fronted the network's religious affairs programme, Sunday, as well as regularly hosting The World At One and The World This Weekend.Stourton was born in 1957 in Nigeria, where his father was working, and also spent time as a boy in Geneva. He was educated at Ampleforth College and read English Literature at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he was President of the Union.His Catholic faith was an important part of his life and Stourton fronted documentaries including Absolute Truth, a four-part BBC Two series about the Catholic Church.It was also reflected in his writing - among Stourton's many books was John Paul II: Man of History, a revelatory biography of the former Pope. His last book, written to coincide with the 250th anniversary of the American Declaration of Independence, was Made in America: the Dark History that Led to Donald Trump.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Edward Stourton photographed at home in 2003Paying tribute, Naughtie praised Stourton's "great integrity and humanity", adding that he "had a kind of infectious decency which lifted people up"."He had a great effect on younger producers, who came away with enormous respect for the extent to which he tried to pass things on," Naughtie added."He was a man of wide cultural interests and experience and enthusiasm... His warm intelligence and wit were admired and enjoyed by generations with whom he worked."And as a friend, he was always a rock of support and kindness. His dignity and courage through his illness were an inspiration to everyone who knew him. He was, simply, much-loved."The Today programme's current editor Rebecca Keating said she and colleagues referred to Stourton as the "Rolls Royce of broadcasting"."His sharp mind and vast knowledge of the world brought to bear on any story with zero fuss," she wrote on X.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Stourton pictured at the Edinburgh International Book Festival at Charlotte Square Gardens in 2018The BBC's interim news chief Jonathan Munro said it was "far too soon to lose Ed"."I worked as his producer when he was ITN's diplomatic editor - unflappable then and ever after," he continued."Many years later, I was listening to his superb analysis of the new Pope for the BBC - another of his many areas of expertise. Not many broadcasters can present Today, BBC One's One O'clock News, The World at One and Sunday - but he did. He leaves a huge gap."Radio 4 controller Mohit Bakaya described Stourton as an "absolutely outstanding broadcaster", adding that he "brought intelligence, authority and an extraordinary hinterland to everything he did"."When Ed was behind the mic, whether on Sunday or the various news programmes he regularly presented for Radio 4, you felt you understood the world that little bit better. Our thoughts are with his family and friends at this sad time."ITV's Julie Etchingham, who also presented Radio 4's Sunday programme, said she was "very sad to hear the news" of Stourton's death, remembering his "huge intellect and great heart".Stourton, who had homes in south London and south-west France, is survived by his wife Fiona, who is a senior television producer, two sons, a daughter and a step-daughter.

schema.headline: Edward Stourton: Broadcaster and journalist dies aged 68

title: Ryanair profits drop as Iran war puts off passengers and lifts fuel costs
description: Ryanair's profits have fallen sharply as war in the Middle East sent jet fuel prices soaring and customers reluctant to book flights.The Irish airline
body: Ryanair's profits have fallen sharply as war in the Middle East sent jet fuel prices soaring and customers reluctant to book flights.The Irish airline's pre-tax profits dropped 34% to €593m (£503m) between April and June while sales were flat as the company was forced to cut fares to stimulate demand.Ryanair also said it expects summer fares to be slightly lower than last year due to "consumer hesitancy" around air travel.The price of fuelling a plane has jumped since the US and Israel launched strikes against Iran in February and while Ryanair said it had "hedged" or struck deals for the most future fuel costs, those not included in these arrangements had more than doubled.  Crude oil prices hit $90 (£67) a barrel for the first time in a month on Monday, before falling back slightly, after a weekend of intense exchanges of fire between the US and Iran.Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz — an essential route for global oil and gas supplies — has ground to a halt. An interim peace deal last month brought some respite to oil and energy prices, but they spiked again as negotiations broke down and fighting resumed.The airline warned that its results for the year will be "highly sensitive" to external factors such as conflict escalation in the Middle East and Ukraine as well as the price of unhedged jet fuel. Fares for for the key summer period between July and September are on track to be "modestly" lower than last year, with many passengers booking closer to departure than normal.The firm's finance chief, Neil Sorahan, said flights on its popular Mediterranean routes were still full. "People [are] as keen to get away as ever, albeit booking just a little bit later," he said.Between April and June, Ryanair's revenue ticked up 1% to €4.4bn.While passenger numbers rose 6% to 6.1 million- helped by the Easter holiday in April - fares fell by 6% as the airline reduced fares to entice flyers concerned about the Iran war.Ryanair's share price fell 5% on Monday.Russ Mould, investment director at AJ Bell, said Ryanair was in a better position than many of its rivals, but nonetheless "visibility is worse than San Francisco airport when the fog sets in"."The renewed escalation in hostilities in the Middle East is unhelpful and without a lasting resolution, challenging times for the airline and travel space look set to continue."

schema.headline: Ryanair profits drop as Iran war puts off passengers and lifts fuel costs

title: The BBC News app keeps you informed with live and breaking news you can trust
description: Depending on the contract you have, data charges may apply for accessing the internet on your mobile device.If you are not sure about the potential ch
body: Depending on the contract you have, data charges may apply for accessing the internet on your mobile device.If you are not sure about the potential charges, please ask your mobile network provider. You may find some costs are included in your existing price plan or that you can opt for a data package that gives reduced charges for accessing the internet.The BBC does not charge you to access mobile content.

schema.headline: The BBC News app keeps you informed with live and breaking news you can trust

title: ‘Everyone's afraid to die’: Struggle to recruit soldiers in Ukraine turns violent
description: Figure caption, Watch: Sarah Rainsford explains why there is violence around conscription in UkraineBySarah RainsfordEastern Europe correspondent Repo
body: Figure caption, Watch: Sarah Rainsford explains why there is violence around conscription in UkraineBySarah RainsfordEastern Europe correspondent Reporting fromKyivVasyl Krupych knows what it is to fight for Ukraine.He describes terrifying scenes from battles in the eastern Donbas region where, sometimes praying, sometimes crying, the machine gunner somehow survived the onslaught by Russian troops.But last December, by then serving in the rear, Krupych was attacked by his own countrymen whilst out searching for draft dodgers.He says two men ignored demands to show their documents and fled. When his patrol gave chase, Krupych was badly beaten with a crowbar. He spent six weeks in hospital."He broke my rib, I was lying there and couldn't breathe," the veteran recalled in a recent interview."What did we fight for, if soldiers can now get beaten?"Image caption, Vasyl Krupych, a serving soldier, was assaulted while out searching for draft dodgersSurge in violenceUkraine's military ombudswoman says such attacks are now "regular" and "a major problem" as the country struggles to mobilise men to fight in the fifth, draining year of Russia's full-scale invasion.It's not a topic people here care to discuss much, wary of feeding Russian propaganda.But the latest National Police Service report recorded more than 600 assaults on recruitment officers to date and warned that the aggression has become "increasingly threatening".The first big alert came in April when several recruiters were stabbed, one fatally. Then this month in Lviv, some 200 protesters surrounded officers attempting to detain a suspected draft dodger and smashed and overturned their car.The riot made headlines as President Volodymyr Zelensky condemned violence against serving soldiers.His controversial decision since then to sack Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov – over a clash of character and vision with Ukraine's commander-in-chief – means attempts to address the conscription crisis will stall.But the anger that just exploded in Lviv suggests that the need is urgent.'Busification'The reasons appear to range from plain fear of the front line – now everyone knows someone who's been injured or killed - to a reluctance to sign up for a war that has no end in sight.The only surge of enthusiasm since the initial rush to the front was in late 2023,  when Ukraine launched a counter-offensive to seize back territory in the south-east.It didn't last.That's when the "brutal mobilisation" phase began, according to opposition MP Oleksandr Honcharenko.He blames that for the backlash."These officers are hunting for people on the streets and sometimes using quite violent methods," the MP says."Violence leads to violence. It's a huge problem and it's going to get worse."Image caption, Ivan Stupak said that Ukraine's high command was forced to use "busification" to tackle a lack of manpower on the battlefieldMilitary analyst Ivan Stupak agrees."We started to suffer a lack of manpower on the battlefield, constant losses," he recalls. "Ukraine's high command had to refill these losses so they started busification."That's the term Ukrainians use for mobile patrols that end up with men bundled into buses, and off to serve."When they need an extra 5,000 men, they start grabbing them - and if they refuse it turns into fights on the street."The professor's storySo for every story of a draft officer attacked, there are accounts of violence by recruiters themselves.Ukraine's Human Rights Commissioner recorded more than 6,000 such complaints last year - double the 2024 tally. They include claims of abuse and unlawful detention as well as poor conditions at recruitment centres.Kyrylo Ogdansky still gets upset when he talks about what happened to him in Dnipro last November.Image caption, Kyrylo was brutally beaten by recruiters despite being exempt from the draftThe university professor, who is exempt from the draft, was approached in the street by a man in plain clothes who wanted to check his call-up status.There are rules about such patrols, including having police officers present, so Ogdansky objected.Then he was assaulted."One of them knocked the phone from my hand and two pushed me into the bus from behind. When I asked what's happening, one started to beat me to the head. He hit me in the nose several times," Ogdansky tells me.He remembers the threat to send him straight to the front line. "He said 'I can call the commander and you'll go to Pokrovsk'."Image source, HandoutImage caption, Kyrylo spent two weeks in hospital following the attack and has made formal official complaintsInstead, the professor spent two weeks in hospital with a suspected neck injury and big purple-black bruises beneath his eyes.He has made formal official complaints but was hesitant about speaking publicly, aware that such things look bad for Ukraine in the middle of a war."Russian propaganda uses such things very actively. But such a problem exists," Ogdansky explains his decision."If everyone is silent, then why would it stop? I understand the problem of conscription, but no official has the right to act like this."Vasyl's storyIt's clear that Vasyl Krupych experienced horrors at war.He volunteered at the very start and has survived some of the grimmest battles.Krupych is proud of his service - a tattoo on his neck declares "Glory to the Ukrainian Armed Forces" – but he suffers from nightmares.When his vehicle was hit by an FPV drone, he was badly injured but managed to drag one friend to safety. He saw two others burned to death. Now, when we discuss the increase in attacks against draft officers, his words drip with hurt.It's not just about what he suffered himself: that case is still going through the courts. It's also the gulf between what he has endured and those doing everything possible to avoid the same."We are doing our duty. We fought, we defended our people - and now they are attacking us."I wonder what he thinks might motivate people to serve, instead of coercing them, and he mentions raising salaries.But then he shrugs."It can't be done differently, it can't. Our lads need to be replaced. But nobody volunteers," he says."Everyone is afraid to die."What to do?In his six months as defence minister, Mykhailo Fedorov did start introducing reforms to tackle the growing crisis.He began with moves meant to make military service more attractive: fixed-term contracts for new recruits, with the option to defer further service, and better salaries, especially for the riskiest roles.But it was an early work in progress and overhauling conscription was still on his to-do list when he was sacked.Proposals have included handing draft patrols over to police, who are less personally invested in the task. There's talk of proper training, too, for officers, and psychological screening.Ukraine does admit openly that it has a problem, which is new.But it now has only an acting defence minister, and still no solution.Additional reporting by Volodymyr Lozhko, Mariana Matveichuk, Anastasiia Levchenko and Sophie Williams.

schema.headline: ‘Everyone's afraid to die’: Struggle to recruit soldiers in Ukraine turns violent

title: When does Andy Burnham become prime minister and what are his policies?
description: A very quick guide to Andy BurnhamImage source, AFP via Getty ImagesByAlex Boyd and Duncan WalkerUpdated 1 hour agoAndy Burnham will become UK prime m
body: A very quick guide to Andy BurnhamImage source, AFP via Getty ImagesByAlex Boyd and Duncan WalkerUpdated 1 hour agoAndy Burnham will become UK prime minister on Monday, replacing Sir Keir Starmer in No 10 Downing Street.Burnham, who became leader of the Labour Party on Friday, will be the fourth prime minister in five years.Monday's events begin with Sir Keir meeting King Charles III at Buckingham Palace, to formally offer his resignation as prime minister. The King will meet Burnham soon afterwards, when he will ask him to form a government. Once Burnham has accepted, he will officially be the UK's prime minister.Burnham will then head to Downing Street, where he is likely to give a speech outside No 10.What does Burnham plan to do as prime minister?Andy Burnham has been fairly tight-lipped about his plans for specific policies, but here's some of what we know:Cost of living: After becoming prime minister, Burnham is expected to lay out plans to give "people breathing space on the cost of living".Digital ID: Burnham will scrap plans for a government-issued digital ID for all British adults, in an attempt to shift focus to "the daily priorities facing people across the country, according to his office.North Sea oil: The new prime minister will also announce plans for new oil and gas drilling in the North Sea. This is likely to only support existing licences rather than issuing new ones, BBC News understands.Thames Water: Burnham has previously said he wants to see "greater public control" of water and energy sectors but has stopped short of calling for full nationalisation. Lenders to Thames Water are preparing to challenge any attempts to nationalise the country's biggest water company.Taxes: Burnham has said there is "some room" for movement on tax. In an interview with LBC, he suggested business rates on warehouses could be increased to fund tax cuts for pubs and some high-street businesses. But he has said he would stick to pledges Labour made not to raise VAT, income tax or National Insurance in its general election manifesto in 2024. He has not ruled out a wealth tax.Local power: A key priority will be to hand more power to councils and authorities away from Parliament. That would involve giving them more control in areas such as housing and transport. As part of that he wants to create another No 10 team over 150 miles north of London, based in Manchester.Who could be in his cabinet?Burnham will want to bring in a new team of ministers.However, he has kept the identity of them very close to his chest.The most intense focus has been on who he will appoint as chancellor to replace Rachel Reeves.Energy Secretary Ed Miliband was long thought to be the most likely candidate, but Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood has emerged as the front-runner.Handing out cabinet jobs is always a balancing act between ensuring the different wings of the party are represented and securing a gender balance in the top team. An all-male line-up in the major cabinet roles would not be a good look.Burnham has appointed James Purnell, a close friend and ally from his last stint in government, to the key backroom role of chief of staff. Former transport secretary Louise Haigh, who ran Burnham's by-election campaign, is also likely to be given a role as a minister in his Downing Street operationWho is Andy Burnham's wife and do they have children?Burnham is married to Marie-France van Heel. They met while they were students at Fitzwilliam College, University of Cambridge, in 1989. They have been together for more than 30 years and married since the early 2000s.Van Heel has worked in marketing and strategy for many years, notably for Sky, and has also been involved in the logo designs for the BBC and England Rugby.The couple have three children - Jimmy, Rosie and Annie.What did he do before becoming prime minister?Burnham was born in Liverpool in 1970 and grew up in Cheshire. He is a lifelong Everton fan and keenly into sports.He studied English at the University of Cambridge and first worked in journalism before moving into politics in his early 20s. He became an MP for the first time in 2001 and held cabinet roles in former prime minister Gordon Brown's government, before eventually standing down as an MP in 2017.During that time, he twice ran for the Labour leadership - he lost on both occasions.Most recently, Burnham was mayor of Greater Manchester, a role he held from 2017 until last month.He drew praise while mayor for his work to transform the region's transport system, and gained a strong reputation as a voice for the north of England.What have other world leaders said about Burnham?Not many international leaders have commented on Burnham's ascent to power.US President Donald Trump has not said much about Burnham so far, but has said he suspects that he is "extremely liberal". The pair are on opposite sides of the political spectrum.Russia said it did not expect a change in its poor relations with the UK with Burnham in charge.British support for Ukraine, and Russian hostility towards the UK, have been the key aspects of recent history between the two countries.In Ukraine, there is some concern about the risk of political instability in Britain. Sir Keir has said the UK's support "will not waver" when the new prime minister takes charge.In the only interview a French official has given on Burnham, Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot wished him well and hoped for "as much stability as possible" in the UK.How did Burnham become PM?Sir Keir won a landslide general election victory two years ago, but began losing public support after a series of policy mis-steps and U-turns.The final straw for many Labour MPs was when right-wing party Reform UK made big gains at local elections in May, leaving many of them fearing Reform leader Nigel Farage would win the next general election.Burnham had long had ambitions to be Labour leader - but could only challenge for the top job as a sitting MP.He returned to Parliament a month ago in a by-election, with his victory over the Reform candidate convincing many Labour MPs he was the right person to replace Sir Keir. Other senior Labour figures - such as former health secretary Wes Streeting - abandoned their own leadership ambitions and got behind Burnham, along with the overwhelming majority of the party's MPs.It meant he became the Labour leader without a contest.In the UK, prime ministers are not directly elected. Unlike US or French presidents, for example, they are the leaders of the party with the most MPs in the House of Commons.This means the UK can change prime ministers without an election - something that has happened with increasing frequency in recent years.Get in touchDo you have questions about Andy Burnham's policies?

schema.headline: When does Andy Burnham become prime minister and what are his policies?
